notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your May income tells a clear story: three client payments totaling $13,960 came in during the month — $8,500 from Figma Design Systems, $4,200 from Notion UX Research, and $1,260 via Stripe. You also made a $4,200 IRS estimated tax payment on April 15, which is a great sign that you're already thinking ahead on this. Since tax matters involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Your estimated tax picture
As a self-employed UX consultant in Texas filing Schedule C, your tax obligations work a bit differently from W-2 employees. Here's the core dynamic:
Texas has no state income tax, which is a meaningful advantage — your tax burden is purely federal. On self-employment income, you owe both the employer and employee share of FICA (Social Security and Medicare), which works out to a 15.3% self-employment tax on roughly 92.35% of your net self-employment income. On top of that, you owe federal income tax on your net profit after the standard deduction and a deduction for half of your self-employment tax.
For a rough sense of scale: based on your $13,960 in income recorded so far this year, the self-employment tax alone comes to approximately $1,972. However, this only reflects what's been recorded in your connected accounts — if your actual annual consulting revenue runs higher, your total obligation grows proportionally.
The quarterly payment schedule
The IRS requires estimated payments four times a year. The 2026 due dates are:
Q1 (Jan–Mar income): April 15 — you paid $4,200 ✅
Q2 (Apr–May income): June 16, 2026 — coming up very soon
Q3 (Jun–Aug income): September 15, 2026
Q4 (Sep–Dec income): January 15, 2027
The June 16 deadline is now less than two weeks away, which makes this a timely question.
How to think about the right amount
Since your consulting income is uneven month to month, two approaches are commonly used:
Safe harbor method: Pay at least 100% of your prior year's total tax liability spread across four equal payments. This protects you from underpayment penalties regardless of how much you earn this year. If your prior year tax was, say, $8,000–$12,000, your quarterly payment would be $2,000–$3,000 per quarter.
Actual income method: Estimate your net profit for each quarter, apply the self-employment tax rate (15.3% on 92.35% of net income) plus federal income tax after deductions, and pay based on what you actually earned. This works well if you track income carefully but requires recalculating each quarter.
Your Ally Tax Reserve Savings account (ending 5391) appears specifically set aside for this purpose, which is excellent discipline. I'd suggest ensuring it holds at least enough to cover the June 16 payment, plus a buffer for Q3.
A practical rule of thumb for your situation
Many self-employed professionals in your profile — solo, no dependents, Texas resident — set aside roughly 25–30% of each client payment into a tax reserve as income arrives. On your $13,960 in May revenue, that would be approximately $3,490–$4,188 in reserve. You've already paid $4,200 for Q1, which suggests your instincts are well-calibrated.
Given the June 16 deadline approaching, would you like to work through what a reasonable Q2 payment might look like based on your income recorded so far this quarter?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
